name: Beta-carotene for skin cancer prevention
summary: Beta-carotene supplementation does not prevent nonmelanoma skin cancer — a well-powered randomized trial found no effect, as did the Nambour trial.
description: The Skin Cancer Prevention Study randomized 1,805 patients with a recent nonmelanoma skin cancer to beta-carotene 50 mg daily or placebo, with 5 years of follow-up. The Nambour trial similarly found no protective effect from beta-carotene.
interpretation: This is an informative negative result: despite a biologically plausible antioxidant rationale, beta-carotene had no effect on new skin cancers (and in other settings high-dose beta-carotene increased lung cancer in smokers). It is included as a comparator to show that not every supplement works — a useful contrast with nicotinamide, which did. See the glossary for confidence interval (the interval here straddles 1, indicating no effect).
methodologyNotes: Effect is the published relative rate with 95% CI for a first new nonmelanoma skin cancer. The topic status is set to inconclusive/neutral to reflect a definitive null from a single large trial (rather than 'limited data').

studies.author: Greenberg
studies.citation: Greenberg ER, Baron JA, Stukel TA, et al. A clinical trial of beta carotene to prevent basal-cell and squamous-cell cancers of the skin (Skin Cancer Prevention Study). N Engl J Med. 1990;323:789-795.
studies.notes: First new nonmelanoma skin cancer; no effect.
